observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the Municipal Accountant to immediately prepare adjusting entries to recognize and record the completed project in the General Fund book of accounts of the LGU amounted to ₱ 4,994,197.72 duly substantiated with complete supporting documents and furnish the Office of the Auditor with certified copies of the same for review and verification.

Further, we recommended to the Municipal Accountant to inform the Barangay Bookkeeper to adjust the same in the book of accounts of Barangay North Poblacion duly supported with complete documentary supports and furnish COA of certified copies thereof for evaluation.

Furthermore, we recommended to the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the BAC Secretariat Head in submitting proof that the lacking bidding documents particularly those that were submitted by the winning contractor upon opening of bid were already handed over to COA for contract review. Otherwise, submit them to the latter immediately for validation.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:
The Municipal Accountant must derecognize the “Construction in Progress – Building and Other Structures” and “Due to LGUs” accounts in the Special Education Fund book of accounts and substantiate it with complete documentary supports and furnish the Office of the Auditor of the same for verification.
The Municipal Accountant must immediately submit a copy of the transmittal letter of the liquidation report stamped received by COA and Provincial Accounting Office and certified copy of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) derecognizing the receivable account from the Provincial Accounting Office to the Office of the Auditor for evaluation. Since the affected accounts were nominal accounts, there is no need for adjusting entries in the Trust Fund.
Hereafter, for every liquidation, the Municipal Accountant must close the liability account or “Due to LGUs” in the books of the LGU upon submission of the liquidation report stamped received by COA and source agency and upon securing the JEV from the source agency in closing its receivable or “Due from LGUs” account.
From this time, the Municipal Treasurer must issue Trust Fund official receipt upon receiving forthcoming fund transfer with specific purpose from other government agencies and deposit it directly in the LGU’s Trust Fund account.
Henceforth, to record all fund transfers from other LGUs with specific purpose in the Trust Fund book of accounts.

We recommended that management expedite the execution of the DOD in accordance with the stipulations outlined in the MOA.
We also recommended to the Municipal Accountant to:
To adjust the eleven completed projects (Projects 1 to 11) currently recorded under CIP and transfer them to their appropriate asset accounts in the TF books while the DOD is still in process.
To derecognize Projects 14 & 15 that were initially recorded in the CIP accounts and make the necessary adjustments. Furnish the audit team with copies of the pertinent documentary supports for verification.
Upon execution of the DOD and securing a copy thereof, immediately correct the ₱ 33,116,492.44 discrepancy in the LGU’s books of accounts and ensure the proper recording of liquidations and the transfer of the assets (Projects 1 to 11 & 17) from Trust Fund to General Fund book of accounts. Submit certified copies of the Journal Entry Vouchers, duly supported by working papers and other pertinent documents, to COA for evaluation.
To implement a systematic and timely approach to updating the “Due to LGUs” account upon submission of liquidation reports, in compliance with IPSAS and COA regulations. This will ensure the financial statements accurately reflect the Municipality's obligations and maintain consistency in inter-agency transactions.
To exercise due diligence in applying accounting entries for implementation of projects by reviewing the Program of Works (POW), MOA and other pertinent documents to determine whether disbursements should be capitalized or expensed outright.

We recommended to the management the following courses of action:
The Municipal Budget Officer to submit a legal basis or the like exempting the other sources of funds of the two (2) supplemental budgets of the LGU which aggregated to ₱ 58.6 million in the LGU’s Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts (SCBAA) final budget in CY 2024. Otherwise, submit a written justification for doing so to the audit team for evaluation.
Hereafter, the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to review and revisit the guidelines on the preparation of SCBAA and include the supplemental budget’s other sources of funds and exclude the reversions of current appropriations in the final budget of the LGU’s SCBAA in the ensuing years.
The Municipal Mayor to direct the Secretary to the Sanggunian to submit a written explanation for the delay in the submission of the hardcopies of the two (2) supplemental budgets in CY 2024 to the Sangguniang Panlalawigan (SP) through the Provincial Local Finance Committee (LFC).
Henceforth, the Municipal Mayor to advise the Secretary to the Sanggunian to submit the hard copy of the future approved appropriation ordinance or supplemental budget of the LGU within three (3) days from its approval to the SP through the Provincial LFC.

We recommended to the Management the following courses of action:
The Municipal Treasurer, with consent of the Municipal Mayor, must send a written order to DBP-Valencia City Branch Manager to stop crediting the interest income of the LGU’s DBP-Valencia City Branch Savings Account directly to the DBP-Valencia City Branch Current Account and let it accrue thereto for easy monitoring of interest earning. Let the LGU transfer the interest earnings from the DBP-Valencia City Savings account to the other bank accounts of the LGU if management has the authority to do so.
The Municipal Treasurer must submit a written agreement or arrangement with DBP-Valencia City Branch relative to the DBP-Valencia City Branch Savings Account under the General Fund and its interest earnings in CYs 2023 and 2024 amounted to ₱ 2.224 million and ₱ 1.988 million, respectively, to the audit team to continue with our post audit.
The Municipal Treasurer must coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to immediately submit copies of the Sangguniang Bayan Resolutions for the newly deposited ₱ 25 million in the DBP-Valencia City Branch Savings Account of the LGU last December 20, 2024 and ₱ 50 million that was re-deposited last December 28, 2023 to the Office of the Auditor for verification.
The Municipal Treasurer must adjust its cashbook for the DBP-Valencia City Branch Savings Account to reflect all the bank transactions in December, 2024 that were not recorded. Hereafter, she must reconcile her cashbook entries with the records of the Municipal Accounting Office regularly.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:
The Municipal Accountant to make an adjusting entry and temporarily use the “Other Receivables” account instead of the “Advances to Contractors” for the advance payments made. She must close it once the goods are completely delivered. She must substantiate the adjusting entry with complete documentation and furnish the audit team with certified copies for verification.
In cases where advance payment is necessary, the LGU must secure prior approval from the President, as required by RA 9184. Moving forward, all requests for advance payment should be carefully reviewed to ensure compliance with existing guidelines.
The management to ensure that payments to suppliers are made strictly in accordance with the terms and conditions stated in the purchase orders (PO).
The MPDC to closely monitor supplier performance to ensure the timely delivery and installation of goods. For overdue items, the MPDC must provide evidence that the supplier requested and was granted an extension by the LGU, and efforts should be made to expedite delivery. Otherwise, the advance payment of ₱ 1,614,889.50 may be suspended or disallowed in audit.
Lastly, the management to submit all missing supporting documents listed in Table 1 and for the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all DVs are supported by complete and appropriate documents before processing payments.

We recommended to the Human Resource Management Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and immediately submit the lacking documentary requirements as cited in Annex E to the Office of the Auditor to continue with the post audit of the disbursements.
For the lacking documents that can no longer be facilitated, we recommended to the management to submit an explanation and justification on why the claim was released/paid without the necessary documents.
Henceforth, all the required supporting documents for claims of terminal leave benefits shall be complete and proper before released or payment thereof.

We recommended to the Local Economic Enterprise Officer to do the following courses of action:
To expedite the final proposal of the new LEEO code. She must consider, among others, the increase in the rental rates of the stalls to keep up with economic inflation and imposition of monetary penalties if there are delinquency with the payments of the rent. She must coordinate with the Municipal Mayor and Sangguniang Bayan for its approval and its immediate release and publication.
To collect the unpaid leases/rents of delinquent lessees. She must coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to establish the unpaid leases/rents as of December 31, 2024 and issue demand letters immediately. She must provide grace period, within the first quarter of CY 2025, for the settlement of the unpaid leases by the delinquent lessees. Otherwise, the latter will not be allowed to enter into a contract of lease if his/her obligation remain unpaid upon expiration of the grace period. Without the contracts, the lessees cannot continue with the leasing of the LGU’s stalls. Also, she must provide the audit team with certified copies of the received demand letters for reference.
To execute contracts of lease to the 413 lessees without contracts and renew the 139 expired contracts of lease once the new LEEO code has been released and published provided that they have no unpaid lease/rent of the stalls. The provisions of these contracts must be based on the new LEEO code. These contracts must be signed by the current Municipal Mayor and the lessees and best if notarized by a lawyer; Furnish the audit team with certified copies of these contracts for reference.
To advertise the twenty one (21) vacant stalls to the general public for prospective or interested applicants.
Hereafter, to execute a contract of lease whenever a stall is awarded to a lessee in the future and strictly adhere to its stipulation especially on its renewal. This legal document shields the LGU from possible infringements by the lessee.
Henceforth, to sternly implement the provisions in the LEEO Code or Municipal Revenue Code particularly on the imposition of increase with the rental rates to help the LGU gain extra revenues to finance priority programs, projects and activities.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:
The Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the Municipal Health Officer to secure a written authority, appointment/designation, certification or the like from the Southern Bukidnon Inter-local Health Zone (SBILHZ) that authorized the former to collect the various LGUs cost sharing and furnish the Office of the Auditor of the same for review and verification;
If recommendation no. 1 is not possible and cannot be complied with, the Municipal Treasurer must immediately turn-over the ₱ 4,650,489.21 duly supported with records or documents bearing complete and relevant details about the fund to the person or member authorized by the SBILHZ to collect and safe-keep it.
The Municipal Accountant must coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to immediately transfer the ₱ 4,650,489.21 from the General Fund to the Trust Fund. The Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) of the transfer must be duly substantiated with complete supporting documents such as but not limited to:
Certified copy from the original of the Subsidiary Ledgers from the year the collections for SBILHZ started up to the end of the year 2024;
Breakdown of the General Ledger account “Trust Liabilities” in the GF as of December 31, 2024 duly certified correct by the Municipal Accountant; and
Other necessary documents that can help verify and validate the total amount to be transferred.
The Office of Auditor must be furnished of the photocopies of the JEV and all the supporting documents.

We recommended to the management to require the barangay management to submit a liquidation report on the financial subsidies they received and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor for review and validation, otherwise, refund the total amount of ₱ 700,000.00 or a Notice of Disallowance will be issued.
Moreover, any subsequent subsidies/assistance given by the LGU to the barangay should be collected by the Barangay Treasurer or SK Treasurer if the fund is to be used for SK purposes and issue an official receipt in favor of the LGU Maramag. Likewise, if the transfer of funds has a specific purpose, it should be supported with a MOA.
Further, the transfer of funds should be recorded as a debit to the account "Due from LGU" for monitoring purposes. Once the barangay liquidates the funds, the amount should be credited back to the same account along with the debit to its corresponding expense account.

We recommended the management to submit a clear and definite legal basis for the payment of the financial subsidy and assistance to the Punong Barangays to the Office of the Auditor for review and validation. Otherwise, refund the total amount of ₱ 360,000.00 or a Notice of Disallowance will be issued.

We recommended to the management to submit the Certification that the procurement involves competitive bidding (53 NOAs) and attendance sheet listing the names of attendees to the BAC meeting.
We further recommended to the management for the additional NOAs together with Certification that same involves competitive bidding and the corresponding attendance sheets to support the over calculation of ₱ 285,747.66, otherwise, refund the said amount.
Also, submit a clear and legal basis for the excessive payment of BAC honoraria to various BAC Secretariat members, otherwise, refund the excess BAC honoraria payment totaling ₱ 53,709.71 to the LGU’s coffer.
Hereafter, we recommended to the management should review the BAC honoraria claims to ensure that no member is paid more than the allowable claims base on the submitted NOAs and 25% of his/her basic monthly salary.

We recommended to the management to submit a letter from the DILG Regional Director approving the foreign travel to Bangkok, Thailand to the Office of the Auditor. Additionally, submit the lacking supporting documents as enumerated in letter B, all for further review and verification of the audit team.

We recommended to the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to confirm the status of the outstanding checks and prepare appropriate adjusting entries to correct the same in the cashbook and book of accounts, respectively. 

Also, the Municipal Accountant should immediately record valid reconciling items as reflected in the Bank Reconciliation Statement (BRS) before closing its accounts for the year to accurately present the “Cash in Bank” account and its corresponding affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant to hasten the work in reconciling the unreconciled/unidentified amounts with assigned subsidiary ledgers of the mentioned general accounts and submit to this office the reconciliation statements/working papers for verification, otherwise;

Follow the procedures in the disposition of dormant accounts such as but not limited to the write-off or derecognition of receivable accounts pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023.

Prepare an accounts payable aging report. Verify the validity of payables and send confirmation letters for balances with identified claimants or creditors. Valid claims shall be paid. If the payable accounts cannot be verified, request for derecognition of the accounts and submit to COA.

Lastly, conduct one-time cleansing for the Property, Plant and Equipment accounts as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.
observations.managementAction:
The LGU have submitted a justification on the matter enclosed with Aging Report of affected accounts, and acknowledged the recommendation for derecognition of the affected accounts.

The LGU has submitted letter request to write-off receivable accounts.

The LGU will implement the necessary one-time cleansing procedures under COA Circular No. 2020-006.
observations.reason: Physical inventory and tagging of affected items are still on-going.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management the following courses of action:

The Municipal Accountant must immediately issue written demand letters to concerned Punong Barangays and Barangay Treasurers on the immediate submission of liquidation reports on the financial assistance given by LGU Maramag. Also, the Municipal Accountant must constantly remind the concerned barangay officials of their responsibility to submit liquidation reports as recipients of funds transferred from LGU Maramag.

The Municipal Accountant must reconcile the unidentified balance amounted to ₱ 34,252.33, otherwise, request for write-off from the Office of the COA. 

3.  Henceforth, the Municipal Mayor must stop from extending monetary grants to barangays with unliquidated financial assistance as of December 31, 2023.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant the following courses of action:

To immediately prepare the adjusting entries to correct the erroneous recording of the 10% LGU counterpart in the General Fund book of accounts and submit certified true copies of the said adjusting entries, subsidiary ledgers of the affected accounts and pertinent documents to support the adjusting entries to the Office of the Auditor to finish the post audit of the said transaction.

Hereafter, to refrain from treating LGU counterpart that requires submission of liquidation report as expense outright; and

Henceforth, to strictly adhere to the provisions stipulated in the forthcoming Memorandum of Agreements (MOAs) of the LGU.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant the following courses of action:

1. To religiously prepare the lapsing schedule for the computation of the depreciation for each component of all of the roads, highways and bridges lodged in the Road Networks account at the end of the year; and

2. To start recognizing and recording accumulated depreciation and depreciation expense at the end of every year pertaining to the Road Networks account.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant to:

1. Prepare a journal entry voucher (JEV) duly supported with the necessary documents such as the foregoing certificates to record the said DA BUB Project amounted to  ₱ 8.18 million in the General Fund book of accounts and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor for review and verification; and

2. Coordinate with the Municipal Engineering Office regularly for the copies of Certificate of Turn-over, Certificate of Acceptance and Commitment and the like to keep abreast with the recognition and recording of Trust Fund Projects that were officially transferred to the LGU in the General Fund book of accounts.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) with working papers and complete supporting documents to adjust the CIP accounts, the appropriate asset accounts and accumulated depreciation of the agency’s General Fund and submit their certified true copies to COA Office. 

Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant must include the foregoing completed projects in the lapsing schedule for the computation of depreciation at the end of the year.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant the following courses of action:

To assess the estimated useful life of the intangible assets; Prepare schedule of amortization and religiously compute for the amortization expense for each component of the Intangible Assets account at the end of the year; and

2.   To start recognizing and recording accumulated amortization and amortization expense at the end of every year pertaining to the “Intangible Assets” account.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:

The Municipal Accountant must immediately prepare an adjusting entries for the ₱ 18.454 million and submit certified copies of the JEV, subsidiary ledgers and other pertinent documents to the Office of the Auditor as shown below:

Aid to Barangays amounted to ₱ 100,000.00

Barangay Project Assistance (BPA) amounted to ₱ 18.354 million

 2. Henceforth, aids to barangays that are already obligated but have no actual fund transfers at the end of the year must be treated as “Timing Differences” specifically “Commitments” in the “Statement of Reconciliation between actual amounts on a comparable basis as presented in this statement and in the Statement of Financial Performance for the year ended December 31, xxxx”  which must be disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements; and

3. Hereafter, the Municipal Accountant must record the aids to barangays, not subject to liquidation, due at the end of the year in “Due to LGUs” account and not in “Accounts Payable” account.
We recommended to the Municipal Treasurer to return the unutilized funds and immediately issue a check payable to the National Treasury amounting to ₱ 2,372,260.00.

We recommended the management to submit a written justification for why they allowed the prices per liter billed by the suppliers above the Province of Bukidnon's Prevailing Retail Pump Price issued by the Department of Energy website to the Office of the Auditor for evaluation. 

Also, submit a written justification for procuring POL products under negotiated procurement instead of public bidding despite the voluminous purchase in CY 2023. 

Henceforth, we recommended to the management to consider the retail pump price issued by the DEO and procure its POL purchase through competitive bidding under GPPB Resolution No. 26-2019, dated October 30, 2019, for the purpose of economies of scale.

We recommended to the management the following courses of action:

To immediately submit a written justification on why it is more efficient and economical for the government to conduct the budget hearing outside the LGU premises to the Office of the Auditor for validation;

To support the procurement through the alternative mode of procurement under lease of real property and venue with justification supporting the lease is more efficient and economical for the government, three (3) RFQs within the vicinity of the selected location and technical specifications under Appendix B of Annex H of the Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184; and

3. To refrain from holding agency activities in expensive venues to avoid disallowance in the audit.

We recommended to the Municipal Treasurer the following courses of action:

To immediately secure proof like validated slip or the like from the treasury section of DPWH Bukidnon 2nd DEO, where remittance of 5% DPWH share was erroneously made, that the ₱ 1,479,428.46 was already remitted to the account of the DPWH Secretary and submit a copy to the Office of the Auditor to finish the post audit of the said transaction; 

Henceforth, to act on her responsibility in remitting the 5% DPWH share to the depository account of the DPWH Secretary; and

Hereafter, to remit the 5% DPWH share directly to the account of the DPWH Secretary maintained by the Bureau of the Treasury as prescribed.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:

To submit a clear and definite legal basis that allows the payment of Personnel Motivation Incentive (PMI) which amounted to ₱ 8,685,390.14 to COA Office to continue with the post audit of the said disbursement; 

If there is a legal basis, submit a written certification to the Office of the Auditor about the sources of appropriation of the PMI deficit amounted to ₱ 80,390.14 and duly support it with pertinent documents; and

3. Henceforth, to stop paying its officials and employees with allowances, incentives and other fringe benefits not covered by existing and specific appropriations and have no clear and definite legal basis to prevent disallowance in audit as provided in COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 30, 2013.
observations.managementAction: The LGU had submitted justification citing legal basis therein.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:

The Municipal Budget Officer should immediately submit the required Obligation Request and Supplemental Budget or Appropriation Ordinance of the refund amounted to ₱ 1,631,480.20 to the Office of the Auditor for further review and validation, otherwise, return/refund the amount of ₱ 1,631,480.20 to the General Fund immediately;

The Municipal Mayor, Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer should submit a written justification/explanation  for the said release of the ₱ 1,631,480.20 in the General Fund, citing the specific sources of funds in the GF, to the Office of the Auditor; and

We further recommended to the management, particularly to the Municipal Engineer, to ensure that the Statement of Work Accomplishment (SWA) must be prepared by the contractor, not by the Municipal Engineer, for every request for payment of infrastructure projects.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:

1.  To submit a clear and definite legal basis and a written justification for the payment of the monthly rice allowance in CY 2022 which amounted to ₱ 8,171,250.00, as of September 2022, to COA Office for further review and validation.

2. To refrain from paying its officials and employees with allowances, incentives and other fringe benefits not covered by existing and specific appropriations and have no clear and definite legal basis to prevent disallowance in audit as provided in COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 30, 2013.

To strictly observe the provision stipulated in DBM Budget Circular No. 2006-1 dated February 1, 2006 in the granting of rice allowance to LGU officials and personnel.

We recommended that the Management undertake the steps provided in the attached COA Circular 2020-006 in order to clean the Property, Plant, and Equipment account of the LGU. Once the one-time cleansing was done instruct the –

Municipal General Services Office to:
Maintain an updated property cards per category of PPE; 
Keep complete records of the PPE of the LGU;
Issue the necessary Property Acknowledgement Receipt once PPE is issued or assigned to an end-user; and
Undertake the annual physical inventory and reconcile with the records of Municipal Accountant’s Office.
Municipal Accountant’s Office to:
Maintain an updated Property, Plant and Equipment Cards for each category of assets; and Real Property Ledger Cards for Land.
Record and disclose the PPE based on the recognition criteria and disclosure requirement set under IPSAS 17 and Government Auditing Manual for LGUs.

Make sure that charges to the budgets are for its intended purpose.

Forward to the BAC the PPMPs after evaluation. 
 
The BAC Secretariat to – 

Consolidate into an indicative APP the evaluated PPMPs with the details required under Section 7.3.4. of the IRR of RA 9184 and with reference to GPPB Circular 07-2015;

Recommend the appropriate procurement modality; and

Follow the submission and posting requirements of APP under GPPB Circular 02-2020 dated May 2020.

We recommend to require the Municipal Accountant to issue the required certification so that additional cash advance cannot be granted to employees with existing unliquidated cash advances.

Cash advances in the total of P1,216,070.95, aged 31 days to 17 years, remained unliquidated as of December 31, 2018.

COA Circular 97-002 provides that liquidations for official travels must be made within 60 days and 30 days for foreign and local travel, respectively. For operating expenses (other than petty cash fund), it must be as soon as the purpose for which it was given has been served. Failure of the AO to liquidate his cash advance within the required period shall constitute a valid cause for the withholding of his salary.

The amount of cash advances stated above where for official travels of the employees and for petty field operating expenses. The same was not liquidated despite the lapse of the required period to settle. Of said amount, P44,972.12 existed and remained dormant for ten (10) years or more and the concerned employees were either retired, transferred or dead.

The same issue was communicated by the previous auditor in his AOM No. 2016-01 (15), as an action thereof, then Municipal Mayor issued an undated Memorandum No. 016 reminding all LGU employees to submit liquidation reports for CY 2014 and previous year’s cash advance, however, the same is still a concern.

The existence of the unliquidated cash advances at year-end means an overstatement of asset account and understatement of the expenses. The amount may not be very material, but the same concern has existed for several years already and was not yet corrected despite the issuance of an AOM and audit recommendations.

We recommended the following:
Require the Municipal Accountant to issue demand letters to the concerned accountable officers for the liquidation of their cash advances, if it is not acted on after a month, the Accountant may withhold the salary until liquidation is made;
The Accountant, after doing the procedures in Section 7 of COA Circular 2016-005, recommend to the Mayor that a request for write-off of dormant accounts be filed to this office for verification and validation;
That the Accountant, as much as possible, make sure that all cash advances be liquidated at year-end to ensure fair presentation of the financial statements;
That the Municipal Mayor issue a Memorandum stating the required period to liquidate the cash advances and for them to faithfully adhere to it.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management instruct the concerned office to submit the returned contracts and purchase orders as well as all contracts awarded in CY 2014 to facilitate legal and auditorial review.

Adhere to the submission of copies of contracts and purchase  orders within five days from issuance as required under COA Circular No. 2009-001, together with its supporting documents forming parts thereof and such other documents peculiar to the contract and/or to the mode of procurement which are deemed necessary in the conduct of auditorial review and in the technical evaluation thereof

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation:
We recommended to constitute an Inventory Team for the purpose of conducting proper and complete physical count of all PPEs of the Agencies, thereafter, require the Accountant to reconcile the result of the count with the accounting records, and to submit a copy of the Inventory and Inspection Report to the Commission on Audit.
We required the Accounting office to properly disclose the changes in the carrying amount of the items in PPE in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare adjustment of the account directly charge to Due to account.  Henceforth, debit only the Due to NGAs; Due to Other NGAs; and Due to LGUs liability account only during liquidation to the source agency.

We have the following recommendations:
To require the Local School Board to conduct proper planning and appropriation of SEF budget to avoid committing the same;
Implement capital outlay projects as soon as possible to avoid using such fund to another program/project/activity.
Refund the amount disbursed with appropriation, otherwise a Notice of Disallowance shall be issued by the auditor.
We recommended to comply with the 1% allocation for LCPC as mandated by RA 9334.
We recommended that the municipality ensure that before the funds are transferred, a Memorandum of Agreement is entered into by LGU Maramag and the implementing agency.
We required that the guidelines of DILB-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 are cautiously complied with to avoid improper charging of disbursements.
We recommended that the agency faithfully adhere to the requirements of project implementation by administration so that the purpose thereof may be achieved.

We required the BAC Secretariat to strictly comply the submission of contracts and purchase orders within the reglementary period of five (5) days after perfection thereof pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001. In addition, ensure that supporting documents are complete in compliance to RA 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct its personnel for the prompt implementation of the projects for the Municipality and its constituents, otherwise return the unspent amount to the funding agencies.
We have recommended to management to regularly conduct physical inventory of its properties and inventories  once a year to determine existence and physical condition of the assets.
observations.managementAction: The conduct of physical inventory is still on going.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II
